naturalLanguage: An Appalachian barn raising is a social group activity: a structured gathering of community members united by reciprocal obligation to contribute labor toward a shared construction goal.

The Haudenosaunee Confederacy (Iroquois League) is a government: a structured political organization with defined roles, laws (the Great Law of Peace), and authority to make collective decisions on behalf of its member nations.

The Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa) is a normative attribute: a set of principles, rules, and governance structures that prescribe how the Haudenosaunee Confederacy shall conduct itself — who holds authority, how decisions are made, and how conflicts are resolved.
The Anishinaabe clan (dodem) system is a social group organization: kinship groups defined by animal totems that carry specific governance roles and responsibilities within Anishinaabe communities.

Aajiiqatigiinniq (deciding together) is a communication act: a structured process of deliberation in which Inuit community members speak, listen, and reach a consensus decision through continued dialogue until all voices have been heard and silence signals agreement.

Pijitsirniq (serving others/the community) is a normative attribute in Inuit culture: a prescriptive principle that positions community service and contribution as the primary ethical obligation of community members — the motivation underlying Aajiiqatigiinniq and other IQ governance practices.

The Foxfire interview and documentation methodology is a cultural practice: a structured approach to preserving community knowledge through student-conducted interviews with elders and community experts, treating community members as the authoritative holders of heritage knowledge.

Community mutual aid (barn raising, cabin raising, harvest days) is a cultural practice: a reciprocal labor-exchange system in which community members contribute work to each other's projects with the expectation of equivalent contribution in return, sustaining community cohesion without monetary exchange.

The Clan Mother role in Haudenosaunee governance is a social role: a defined position with specific authority (selecting and removing chiefs, holding Confederacy membership) and responsibilities within the Haudenosaunee political structure, transmitted matrilineally within clans.
